text: The surgeon evaluates the transabdominal anatomy, revealing a large indirect hernia with no visceral involvement. The peritoneal flap is developed starting high around the arcuate line, with dissection progressing laterally to reflect tissue up to the side wall, ensuring entry into the visceral compartment.

The surgeon proceeds with the reduction of a large, thin hernia sac, utilizing gentle manual pressure applied by the bedside assistant over the groin. A force bipolar instrument is used in the left hand to modulate grip strength during the reduction process.

The surgeon uses a stronger grasp to reduce slippage and prevent tearing of the hernia sac while pulling it out of the canal, reflecting the spermatic vessels and vas deferens. The sac is transected from the remaining adhesions and tissue. The surgeon then establishes the critical view of the myopectineal orifice, starting medially and developing the retropubic space. For indirect hernias, the surgeon ensures at least two centimeters of space between the pubis and bladder, carefully finding an areolar plane to avoid disrupting vessels.

The surgeon works on parietalization of the spermatic vessels and the vas, carefully managing the fat emanating from the femoral canal. Some fat is transected and left as a plug within the canal to avoid disrupting lymphatics and causing bleeding. The surgeon aims to achieve adequate parietalization to lay the mesh flat, while ensuring the spermatic cord structures are properly lateralized.

The surgeon adjusts the heavyweight, extra-large mesh to ensure adequate lateral coverage and proceeds with fixation to Cooper's ligament using an air knot. The mesh is secured with multiple fixation points for direct hernias, followed by a standard flap closure using a nine-inch V-lock suture. Insufflation pressure is reduced, and the flap is closed without fenestrations.

The surgeon assesses the flatness of the mesh in the preperitoneal compartment by closing the flap, dropping the insufflation with the camera inside, and then quickly re-insufflating. The pressure gradients between the preperitoneal and intraperitoneal spaces allow the peritoneum to reflect back, confirming that the mesh is not tenting and appears flat.
